summary.scholar: By the early 1790s, disagreements over the direction of the new nation led to the formation of America's first political parties. Alexander Hamilton led the Federalists, who favored a strong central government and close ties with Britain. Thomas Jefferson and James Madison led the Democratic-Republicans, who favored states' rights and sympathy with France. George Washington warned against political parties in his Farewell Address.
summary.explorer: Even though George Washington warned against it, America split into two political teams in the 1790s! Alexander Hamilton led the Federalists, who wanted a strong government. Thomas Jefferson led the Democratic-Republicans, who wanted states to have more power. This was the beginning of the two-party system we still have today!

significance.scholar: The emergence of political parties, though unintended by the Founders, became a permanent feature of American democracy and shaped the nation's political development.
significance.explorer: America has had two major political parties ever since! The names have changed, but the idea of two teams competing for votes started right here.

sources.citation: Miller Center: U.S. Presidents and Key Events
